content:
# Mark Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 33r–33v (PDF pages 65–66) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_004_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_004_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ Again he began to teach beside the sea. A very large crowd gathered around him, so that he got into a boat and sat on the sea, while the whole crowd was on the shore facing the sea.

² He taught them many things in parables, and in his teaching he said to them:

³ "Listen! A sower went out to sow.

⁴ And as he sowed, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it.

⁵ Other seed fell on rocky ground where it had little soil. It sprang up quickly because the soil was shallow,

⁶ but when the sun rose it was scorched, and since it had no root, it withered away.

⁷ Other seed fell among thorns, and the thorns grew up and choked it, and it yielded no crop.

⁸ And other seed fell into good soil, and it grew up and increased and bore fruit—thirtyfold, sixtyfold, and a hundredfold."

⁹ And he said, "He who has ears to hear, let him hear."

¹⁰ When he was alone, those around him with the twelve asked him about the parables.

¹¹ He said to them, "To you has been given the mystery of the kingdom of God, but for those outside, everything comes in parables,

¹² ⚠️ so that 'they may indeed see but not perceive, and may indeed hear but not understand, lest they should turn and be forgiven.'"

¹³ And he said to them, "Do you not understand this parable? How then will you understand all the parables?

¹⁴ The sower sows the word.

¹⁵ These are the ones along the path where the word is sown: when they hear, Satan immediately comes and takes away the word sown in them.

¹⁶ And these are the ones sown on rocky ground: when they hear the word, they immediately receive it with joy.

¹⁷ But they have no root in themselves, and they endure only for a time. When tribulation or persecution arises on account of the word, they immediately fall away.

¹⁸ And others are the ones sown among thorns: they hear the word,

¹⁹ but the cares of the world, the deceitfulness of riches, and the desire for other things enter in and choke the word, and it proves unfruitful.

²⁰ But those sown on good soil are the ones who hear the word, accept it, and bear fruit—thirtyfold, sixtyfold, and a hundredfold."

²¹ He said to them, "Is a lamp brought in to be put under a basket, or under a bed? Is it not put on a stand?

²² For nothing is hidden except to be made manifest, nor is anything secret except to come to light.

²³ If anyone has ears to hear, let him hear."

²⁴ And he said to them, "Pay attention to what you hear. With the measure you use, it will be measured to you—and still more will be added.

²⁵ For to the one who has, more will be given; and from the one who has not, even what he has will be taken away."

²⁶ And he said, "The kingdom of God is as if a man should scatter seed on the ground.

²⁷ He sleeps and rises, night and day, and the seed sprouts and grows—he does not know how.

²⁸ The earth produces by itself: first the blade, then the ear, then the full grain in the ear.

²⁹ But when the grain is ripe, at once he puts in the sickle, because the harvest has come."

³⁰ And he said, "With what shall we compare the kingdom of God, or what parable shall we use for it?

³¹ It is like a grain of mustard seed, which, when sown on the ground, is the smallest of all the seeds on earth,

³² yet when it is sown, it grows up and becomes the largest of all garden plants, and puts out large branches, so that the birds of the air can nest in its shade."

³³ With many such parables he spoke the word to them, as they were able to hear it.

³⁴ He did not speak to them without a parable, but privately to his own disciples he explained everything.

³⁵ On that day, when evening had come, he said to them, "Let us cross over to the other side."

³⁶ Leaving the crowd, they took him with them in the boat, just as he was. Other boats were with him.

³⁷ A great windstorm arose, and the waves broke over the boat, so that it was already filling with water.

³⁸ But Jesus was in the stern, asleep on the cushion. They woke him and said, "Teacher, do you not care that we are perishing?"

³⁹ He awoke, rebuked the wind, and said to the sea, "Peace! Be still!" The wind ceased, and there was a great calm.

⁴⁰ He said to them, "Why are you so afraid? Have you still no faith?"

⁴¹ And they were filled with great awe and said to one another, "Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?"

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

1. **"Parables" vs. "stories" (v. 2):** EMET and LOGOS use "parables." PNEUMA uses "stories." I retain "parables" as a term readers expect in this context and which carries specific technical meaning in biblical studies—a comparison drawn from daily life to illustrate a spiritual truth.

2. **v. 12 — purpose clause:** All three translators preserve Mark's purposive ΙΝΑ ("so that"). This verse is flagged ⚠️ because the translators diverge in how they frame the theological difficulty in their notes, though the translation itself is consistent.

3. **"Fall away" (v. 17):** EMET: "are-scandalized." LOGOS: "fall away." PNEUMA: "give up." I adopted LOGOS's "fall away" as the clearest rendering of σκανδαλίζονται in context.

4. **v. 40 — "afraid" vs. "cowardly":** EMET renders δειλοί as "cowardly." LOGOS softens to "afraid." PNEUMA goes strong with "such cowards." I adopted LOGOS's softer "afraid" — while noting that the Greek is indeed stronger — because the point is the contrast between fear and faith, not an accusation of moral failure.

5. **v. 39 — the calming command:** EMET: "Be-silent! Be-still!" LOGOS: "Peace! Be still!" PNEUMA: "Quiet! Be still!" I adopted LOGOS's "Peace! Be still!" as the most resonant English rendering, capturing both the authority and the result.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### Verse 12 — Purpose of the Parables

**The issue:** Does Jesus teach in parables *in order to* conceal (purposive, following Mark's ΙΝΑ) or *with the result that* outsiders do not understand (resultative)?

- **EMET** translates "in-order-that" (strict purposive), preserving the scandal of the Greek.
- **LOGOS** translates "so that" (purposive but slightly softer), noting that Matthew changes this to "because" (ΟΤΙ).
- **PNEUMA** translates "so that" (purposive), preserving the difficulty but contextualizing it with the Isaiah quotation.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three agree on the purposive force. The consensus translation reads "so that" — faithful to Mark's ΙΝΑ. The verse is flagged because this is one of the most theologically debated passages in Mark. The reader should be aware that this saying, placed on Jesus's lips, asserts that parables simultaneously reveal (to insiders) and conceal (from outsiders). Whether Mark intends divine predestination or dramatic irony is a question each reader must weigh.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
